one_line_takeaway: AgentBound brings Android-style declarative permissions plus a Docker sandbox to MCP servers, auto-generating manifests (80.9%-96.5% accurate) and blocking system-resource attacks with sub-millisecond runtime overhead, but it cannot stop misuse that stays inside the granted policy.
key_finding: AgentBound blocks the system-resource-targeting attacks it is designed for while imposing near-zero steady-state cost. In the security experiments it prevented all 9 attacks that break out of the access-control boundary (e.g., SSH-key exfiltration, Google Maps and weather servers rewriting API hosts to a malicious IP), and by design could NOT stop attacks that stay within granted permissions (the B.2 puppet attack redirecting a crypto transfer to a still-permitted endpoint, the B.4 wechat SQL injection, and pure LLM-targeting prompt injections C.1/C.6). Auto-generated manifests were 80.9% accurate/precise with 100% recall per developer review, and 96.5% accurate (precision 0.94, recall 0.96) versus hand-written references, with the capability vocabulary covering 100% of real-world server needs. Runtime overhead after startup was 0.6 ms (macOS) / 0.29 ms (Debian) on average; only container startup added a few hundred ms.
core_contribution: AgentBound is presented as the first access-control framework for Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, the components that give LLM agents access to files, networks, and OS resources. It has two parts the authors build themselves: (1) AgentManifest, a declarative, Android-permission-inspired policy that lets an MCP server declare the generic capabilities it needs (an 18-entry vocabulary across five categories: filesystem, system, network, peripherals, others), plus AgentManifestGen, a two-stage LLM pipeline that auto-generates a manifest from a server's source/docs; and (2) AgentBox, a Docker-based policy enforcement engine that wraps an unmodified MCP server in a container and enforces the manifest via filesystem mounts, iptables outbound allow-lists, and environment-variable whitelists (deny-by-default). The capability vocabulary was systematically derived by mapping 16 AI-agent use-cases from a literature survey (11 papers) onto Android permissions, then intersecting with the macOS TCC model (pruning mobile-only permissions, merging fine/coarse capabilities).
threat_model: The adversary controls one or more malicious/compromised MCP servers and aims to compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of the AI-agent system. The agent itself is assumed to behave as intended but is vulnerable to attacks embedded in prompts, tool descriptions, or returned data. Building on Song et al.'s taxonomy, the paper deliberately scopes to SYSTEM-RESOURCES-TARGETING attacks (those that read/modify filesystem, network, or OS interfaces and are thus mitigable by runtime access control): MCP prompt injection, tool poisoning, puppet attacks, rug-pull attacks, and MCP application-level attacks (e.g., SQL injection, supply-chain, runtime API-host rewriting). Explicitly OUT OF SCOPE: attacks that only manipulate the LLM's reasoning or output without touching system resources, and misuse that stays within the granted policy boundaries (the acknowledged 'semantic gap').
limitations: By construction AgentBound cannot prevent attacks that operate within the declared policy: a puppet/tool-poisoning attack that only alters a parameter of an already-permitted network call (B.2), or an application-level flaw such as SQL injection in the server's own legitimate DB access (B.4). Pure LLM-reasoning manipulations that never touch system resources are out of scope. Enforcement depends on Docker containerization and coarse device access (camera/mic mounts are all-or-nothing; fine-grained device/clipboard/notification/location control is only sketched via a hypothetical signed companion app). The manifest quality rests on LLM generation and still needs human review (worst case 'Clerk' at 76.5% accuracy; most mismatches were false-negatives/omitted capabilities). Security evaluation is small-scale (one hand-made server, 4 + 10 public malicious servers), and 74% of the 96 filed GitHub issues received no maintainer response, limiting the developer-validation signal.

datasets_used: PulseMCP top-300 MCP servers by GitHub stars (296 downloadable; 59-63,215 stars) - authors' own dataset
Subset of 96 servers filed as GitHub issues for developer review
Subset of top-48 servers with author hand-written reference manifests
Song et al. public malicious-MCP dataset (4 servers B.1-B.4: Google Maps, mcp_server_time, mcp-weather-server, wechat-mcp)
Public MCP security-challenge dataset (10 malicious servers C.1-C.10)
quantitative_results: Capability vocabulary: 18 capabilities in 5 categories, derived from 16 literature use-cases (11 papers)
Developer review: 80.9% accuracy and precision, 100% recall; of 96 issues 74% no response, 17.7% accepted correct+complete, 4.2% under discussion, 4.2% rejected

Manual reference comparison (48 servers, 816 capabilities): 787 matched = 96.5% accuracy, precision 0.94, recall 0.96; 28/48 servers at 100%; worst case Clerk 76.5%; 23/29 mismatches were false negatives, 6 false positives
Capability prevalence (AgentManifest vocab, 296 servers): network.client 83.1%, system.env.read 79.6%, filesystem.read 74.1%, filesystem.write 49.3%, network.server 30.6%
Capability prevalence (Android vocab): internet 90.5%, read_external_storage 50.3%, write_external_storage 40.8%
Security: 9 system-resource-targeting attacks prevented; non-preventable = B.2 puppet, B.4 SQL injection, C.1/C.6 prompt injection into LLM, C.7 app-level not targeting system resources
Startup overhead: macOS ~150-300 ms, Debian up to ~400 ms (e.g., Google Maps macOS 237.4 ms sandboxed vs 79.2 ms native)
Steady-state runtime overhead: 0.6 ms avg (macOS), 0.29 ms avg (Debian)
AgentManifestGen API cost: $99.25 for both capability systems
93.3% of MCP server implementations written in JavaScript or Python (cited motivation for desktop/TCC focus)
baselines_compared: Native (unsandboxed) MCP server execution vs. AgentBox sandbox for both security (mode A.1) and efficiency (startup + runtime)
AgentManifest capability vocabulary vs. full Android Manifest permission system for auto-generation coverage
AgentManifestGen auto-generated manifests vs. author hand-written reference manifests
Isolation configurations: native (A.1) vs. network-blocked (A.2) vs. fully sandboxed filesystem+network (A.3)
